topic: An employee is transferred to a different department
raw_output:
[story 1]

The cardboard box felt impossibly heavy in Elias's hands. He stared at the beige cubicle wall, the fluorescent lights buzzing with an irritating, high-pitched whine that seemed to vibrate directly in his molars. Moving boxes, paperwork, the entire migration to the Acquisitions team—it all felt like wading through thick, lukewarm honey. He dragged his feet across the carpet, the movement slow, each step requiring a conscious effort against a heavy inertia. When his manager, Sarah, clapped him on the shoulder, his response was a faint, almost inaudible mumble, his eyes unfocused, as if his brain hadn't quite caught up with the instruction. "Welcome aboard, Elias," she chirped, oblivious to the internal resistance radiating from him. He just nodded, the motion stiff, already anticipating the long, drawn-out days ahead.

[story 2]

I kept blinking, trying to force my eyes to register the new layout of the floor plan. The air in the open-plan office felt thick, almost viscous, clinging to my skin. Every task seemed magnified, each required interaction a monumental undertaking. My fingers hovered over the keyboard, the keys feeling distant and unresponsive. I remembered the morning meeting, the rapid-fire discussion about Q3 projections, and now, nothing seemed to anchor itself. I shuffled toward the designated area for the new team, the sound of my own footsteps echoing disproportionately loud in the sudden quiet. "Morning," I managed, the word emerging as a low, gravelly sound, utterly devoid of its usual morning energy. I just wanted the day to pass, quietly, without any real engagement.

[story 3]

The transfer memo sat on my desk, a stark white rectangle mocking my current state of inertia. I picked it up, the paper feeling strangely limp. Outside the window, the city traffic flowed with a relentless, unhurried pace, mirroring the rhythm inside my own chest. I spent a good ten minutes just staring at a dust mote dancing in a sunbeam, watching it drift with excruciating slowness. When Mark from the new department approached, offering a friendly wave, I managed a small, tight smile that didn't quite reach my eyes. "Yeah," I replied, the tone flat, like a recording played at half speed. I felt weighted down, as if moving required the expenditure of immense, unnecessary fuel. I decided, quite simply, to just sit down.
